sentences.text: I went to the parson of the church.
He was my first contact, and told him that first-- that I have to go to another contact.
He was in charge of passport bureau.
So he told me, look, he just left.
He went to Grodno, it seems to me.
And there is another man.
So you have to do it on your own.
This was the first hurdle.
So I decided that I cannot take Sula there, due to her appearance.
I went there to him.
I said-- presented two photographs, and whatever or not, that I want a passport.
I came here.
I am a gardener.
I intend to do.
Where do you come from?
So I told him that I came from [NON-ENGLISH], some kind of a village, whatever or not.
And so-- because here, I know that some Jewish farmers were taken away.
So I better do this work.
So-- and so-- I gave him these documents.
And in-between, I put in a greenback, $100.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I even-- it didn't take even the whole 15 minutes.
And I had the passport.
So I went back.
Further contacts-- it turned out that one thing is AK, it means Home Army, the staff in the city was generals, educated officers, with clear ideology of what they have to do.
And the other thing is the field part of it.
First of all, the parsons of the church could not make the contact.
Secondly, when he made the contact with the Colonel Slaski from the Nowogrodek region-- oh, all right, one name passed.
So they-- he told him that he doesn't-- that the whole area belonged to him.
And he doesn't want any new units in western part of the Vilna area and that he is not going to cooperate.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So this was, to me, it's a bad [INAUDIBLE].
Anyway, look, the parson probably contacted whom he meet in Vilna.
And then-- and he gave me work to prepare firewood for the church and for the parsonage.
By the way, he, he, too, a Jew from assimilated family in Vilna.
Huh?
That I was Jewish?
I-- he treats me as a Pole and as an officer, you know.
He would always-- would always tell me-- would always tell me, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mr.
Mielzynski.
I told you it was my name, you know, how I came to it.
Well, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] so I worked for-- and then winter set in.
It was terrible cold, you know.
I don't remember such a winter.
Maybe such a winter was somewhere around '28, '29, when I was still in the high school.
And, like, so Germans-- and they started to go in, in the ghetto, in the shoe factory in the ghetto.
They made for them special boots out of special prepared-- I don't know how to call it-- heavy material, you know, you know.
And, and one of-- one such an officer came in-- into my house and, and looked at my wife, said, oh, what a beautiful [NON-ENGLISH] girl, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH] Frau.
When it was evening time, my parents escaped from the ghetto and came to me.
I said, it's very dangerous.
What are you doing?
And the worse of thing, they put in a White Russian by the name Taratuta, who is a pregnant wife.
And believe me or not, she bore a child, a girl.
And I had to deliver.
I didn't know how.
So Sula was behind me, you know.
She told what to prepare, hot water, whatever or not, I don't know.
And she used tell me, step by step, what I have to do.
And you know that, when I was arrested, that Taratuta's wife and Taratuta's child, the girl, lived.
Nothing.
I just-- I just kept hacking firewood.
And then one, one day in December, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] came a, a gentleman in sleds.
He introduced himself, I am so-and-so.
And I know that you are a gardener.
And I am a director of an estate, Zapole.
And I would like you should garden for me.
It is-- it's under the auspices of the German army, he said.
But I told him that we are going to organize their garden and hothouses.
And where do you live?
He came, you know, in a-- that small, personal sleds with one horse.
He said, tomorrow I am coming here with men.
I will take you away.
I came there the next day.
He came in.
We loaded everything.
We tried to, you know, to keep, our child, you know, warm.
He didn't look at her, you know.
He didn't look at me, and nothing more.
Whether or not, I start to organize-- what I know-- I was-- I have no-- I don't know the English name for the unit which is a connection between-- it is not the signal corps.
What you call it, you know?
But I was in, in the charge of the district Lida West.
And through my hands had to go through all newcomers to AK.
Now, the field AK consisted of a variety of political ideological groups.
And through my bad luck, I had all the worst people that could be there.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I organized it the way I understood.
And then I-- the word came that I should, as a geographer-- in the future, they planned an attack on Vilna.
And I should prepare plans what to do.
And they passed to me certain papers, which showed you which points in Vilna were fortified.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In, in about May '45, my mother, dressed as a peasant, came to me and told me that Rewenska was arrested.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I already probably told you this story.
Yeah, so I don't repeat it.
And, all right-- and she went back.
And then I know that there's even more I didn't know.
What else was-- who else was arrested?
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that I should be very careful.
And I tried to describe it-- was very careful.
But, no, I tried to find out what was all around.
And little by little, I organized.
I knew who is who, where is located, where we can get arms, what kind of arms.
I acquired-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] yeah, but I didn't know-- I used to steal vodka, you know.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because this was the best-- the best-- to get arms.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I had two kinds of it-- three kinds of it.
One for the regular soldiers, one for a little bit higher, and one-- it was a real, very good vodka, without anything, you know.
It would go to the officers.
They knew that we did it.
But they-- no, they looked through their fingers.
No Jews.
All Poles, all Poles, and highly anti-Semitic.
One day, came up through our hands one reserve officer by the name Pilch.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He was sent from-- no, he, he was-- he was a parachutist.
He parachuted into Poland from London and from Polish.
And they sent him to part-- to that Colonel Slaski to organize.
Now, he had to go through my and Krzanowski's hands.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I asked him, what is the main task of the AK?
So without thinking about it, he said, the main task is to fight Judo-Commune.
And then of course, he says, Germans.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
How do you like it?
So I gave him a, a lecture.
I told him, which I repeated that many times, that let Germans do their work with Jews.
It takes away attention from us.
And you fight Germans.
That Pilch went to Nowogrodek to Stolpce, a small town near Nowogrodek, took over the command.
And I don't know what-- how-- what had happened, he got in a row with the-- some of the partisans.
And he really-- he had a alliance with Germans, got from them-- got from them arms, and started to fight not Germans, but he started to fight Jews and Soviet partisans.
You know, many years ago, Channel 13 had a program about Polish history.
In one of the sections, third or fourth, to my big surprise, I-- Pilch from London came to speak for AK.
I was so enraged, you know.
I jumped up.
I didn't believe, you know.
And he looked almost the same way as he looked, you know, 40 or 50 years ago.
So I wrote a letter to-- I wrote a letter to Channel 13.
And they contacted me with producers of the film.
And I told them what I think-- that they could find a better representative for AK.
Because on account of-- I told them, on account of such son-of-a-bitches like Pilch that thousands of good, Polish people perished.
Because Soviets never forgot what AK, certain units of AK, did to them.
Were you at the Zapole estate for the rest of the war?
Or did you leave at some point?
Let me finish up.
The 42nd year-- 1942 somehow went through.
And there was a terrible thing what the Germans did to Lida Ghetto.
In May of '42, I think it was the 8th, they surrounded the ghetto.
A couple of weeks before, they took out all Jews which were in Lida Ghetto from Vilna, all the Vilna Jews, and shot them, something like 60, 70 people.
I don't know exactly, you know.
Then I remembered that on that day-- day before, I went-- I had to do some contact.
So I went to that parson-- to that parson.
The Slaski-- the Colonel Slaski from the Nowogrodek district was a son of a bitch, too-- anti-Semite.
And he didn't do anything about Lieutenant Pilch, despite the fact that the, the main command from Warsaw wrote him a letter about his-- activities of some of his officers, you know.
But at that time, I separated his group.
And with no-- any information, which we used to do, would go to Nowogrodek.
We, we, we were afraid, you know, of being betrayed there.
So on that day, you know, they surrounded the ghetto and took out a couple thousand Jews, and shot them in Ponary.
Not in Ponary, in a place called Kaszary.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Three or four days before, Jews dug there a big ditch.
And I never will forget, you know, in the morning, I was going back about 9 o'clock, you know.
I slept over in Parson's house.
I was going back.
And I-- and suddenly, I was diverted from that road by a-- by, yeah, Polish policemen, you know, who worked for Germans, and some German soldiers.
He said, you take the side road, don't-- two kilometers back.
I said, to go a side road back?
And then I start to hear shooting, you know.
So I turned into forest.
And through the trees, you know-- they had a ditch.
And on the ditch, such a bark.
Behind the bark, a machine-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I already-- Anyway, women, children, young, old were put on them and rat-tat-tat-tat-tat.
And they'd-- [SIGHS] I, you know, I came home.
And I couldn't tell it to Sula because in Lida Ghetto was her father.
I didn't know who they had taken.
But I told Krzanowski what I saw.
So we kept quiet.
And then in the evening, peasants came and told her this story.
Do you know that the Jews came, and they were told to cover up this?
And the earth heaved because people were living there.
They, they covered them with lye and covered them with earth.
And, and for three or four days, from-- everyone would come and tell this story, each story more tragic, more horrible, you know, than the-- you know, Sula became very upset, you know.
So the next day-- the next week, probably, I contacted some ghetto, and told me, Staropolski's alive-- that was that Staropolski's-- is alive.
And so this-- so we organized completely.
And I made the plan that-- to be submitted to Vilna to Colonel-- oh, I forgot his name.
I don't know.
I know his guerre-- nom de guerre, Wilk-- 'wolf.' Now, that is a way because the eastern side was very strongly fortified by several bunkers, you know, bunkers, and so on.
So they should try to transfer a majority of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] partisans on the left flank, you know, and make a diversion attack from the east.
And the main attack from the west, where there was only one strong point, you know, from the west.
And not to touch, you know, the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] no, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] where the-- where the planes land, you know.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then it started to-- in-- I learned that, in, in the forest Rudniki and the forest Nacha, one is about 25 kilometers from Vilna to the southwest.
And the other, probably some 60 or 70 kilometers-- are Jewish partisans.
But I organized there a group Polish partisans.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then it was probably in June '42, working, you know, in the hothouse or the hotbeds, you know, over that-- I saw airplanes [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bombarding the Nacha-- the Nacha Forest here going-- no, they, they came from, from Vilna airfield-- Porubanek-- bombarded Rudniki Forest, flew south, bombarded, bombarded Nacha Forest.
And then around it-- and again, you know.
There were three planes, taking-- it seems.
This I much remember.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And then a couple days later, one of the officers, whom I put in charge, tells-- comes to me and said, well, I, I had a-- I did a good deed.
I said, what happened?
Oh, he said, you know, day before yesterday, when they bombarded, bombarded Rudniki Forest and the Nacha Forest?
So I put my men and cut off all the-- all the exits from the forest.
And, he said, and all the kikes perished there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, I lost-- and I bawled him out.
You know, first of all, I started to act formally.
Attention!
I said.
Get up!
He couldn't understand I said that.
So I said, so, you know, he was sitting all-- I said, attention!
I said.
And I gave him a lesson.
He said, what happened? he said.
So they killed-- I am not sure, he said.
But maybe they killed a couple of Jews there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This what has happened, you know.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, what has happened to people in Rudniki Forest and Nacha Forest was described.
So that is all.
They had a very hard time.
Not so much with the AK members, but with the local population.
Because, you know, they foraged for food.
And peasants didn't want to give, you know.
And there is not enough money there, you know, with people.
Because who, who are those?
Not the fat, Jewish, rich man from Vilna, but young boys and girls, you know.
So it wasn't-- they didn't have anything.
They had nothing.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
There was a lot of things, you know.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [BACKGROUND NOISE] At the end of August 1943, they sent a thresher.
You know, in '42, when they had took off the grain, you know, harvested grain, they flailed it.
You know what's a flail?
It is such a stick with another thing, and you beat the heads of the, you know, beat our grain.
The Germans sent a thresher, a mechanical thresher, which worked on a-- on a tractor.
It was the first time that they saw a tractor on, on the Zapole Estate.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it-- and it-- and we started to thresh grain.
And as-- to guard the tractor and the thresher, they, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they sent three policemen and a German soldier-- four people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The thresher-- the tractor kept kept broke-- to break down-- two or three times.
So whatever had to be done in three or four days lasted and lasted into-- you know.

The very first day when they came, I-- the very first day when they came, I took Sula and took her to a safe place, to a village called Rulewicze about 10 kilometers, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] probably south or southwest from, from the place where we were, Zapole.
But when I came back, I told that she and Zarek went to her friend's, to village [NON-ENGLISH], which was just on the opposite side, you know, opposite side of Lida.
One day, one of the-- I was working in grading tomatoes, the policeman came up and started to play with the revolver, and so on, talking about this and that, and whatever it is.
And he says, and I had the very good relations with the villages-- village people.
I went, you know, and I matched a couple there, who with full Polish customs, whatever or not.
And he came, you know, he tells me-- you know, he tells me with a, a smile-- I was in working clothes-- he said, you look like a Jewish butcher.
God, I heard that, you know.
So, you know, I was-- I almost dropped dead.
I didn't know what to do.
I looked at him.
I look at him.
I thought that I'd rather-- and finally, the only thing I can do is to, to play [INAUDIBLE], you know.
And I swore-- you know, I can't repeat it here.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, in Polish or in Russian, it's called a three-- a three-story swearing, you know.
It was-- so he looked at me.
And, you know, he goes, no, I didn't think-- I didn't mean anything, you know.
It was just-- it was just, you know, it occurred to me that you look this way.
But no, he says.
You know, when he said so, I didn't like it.
And I started to think that I have to get out from this.
I don't know for how long.
We'll see what happens.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Sometimes-- in September '43, the 2nd or the 3rd, I went to sleep over with my wife, you know, to the village Rulewicze because I want to know-- whatever.
I brought-- I brought milk.
I brought butter, you know, other things.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And suddenly at night, I was awakened up by shooting.
I looked up, and I saw tracers, you know, right in the direction of Zapole.
I couldn't believe it.
I-- early in the morning, I ran and found a tremendous [INAUDIBLE].
That policemen ran.
German soldiers were, was killed.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this means that they simply murdered the whole village.
OK?
